g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about cache memory, a critical component that bridges the speed gap between CPU and RAM.
The CPU processes data thousands of times faster than RAM can supply it, creating a performance bottleneck.
g.text: The von Neumann bottleneck slows computation significantly

g.data-narration: Cache memory is a small, fast buffer that stores frequently used data, dramatically reducing CPU wait time.
The memory hierarchy arranges storage by speed and size: registers, cache, RAM, and storage in a pyramid.
Cache works on locality: temporal means re-accessing same data soon; spatial means accessing nearby data.
When the CPU needs data, it checks cache first: a hit provides fast access, a miss requires slower RAM fetch.
Hit ratio measures cache performance: higher ratio means more efficient caching, typically 90% in modern systems.
Direct mapping assigns each memory block to one cache line, simple but can cause conflict misses.
Fully associative mapping allows any block in any cache line, flexible but requires expensive hardware search.
Set associative mapping divides cache into sets, each with multiple lines, balancing cost and performance.
g.text: Set maps to any of 4 lines

g.data-narration: L1 cache is the fastest, built into the CPU core, split into instruction cache and data cache.
L2 and L3 cache are larger and slower but still much faster than RAM, often shared between CPU cores.
Write-through policy updates both cache and RAM simultaneously, ensuring consistency but slower writes.
Write-back policy writes only to cache initially, updating RAM later when the line is evicted.
Cache memory uses locality principles to store frequently accessed data, dramatically improving CPU performance.
Modern CPUs use L1, L2, L3 caches with set-associative mapping and write-back policies for optimal performance.
Thank you for learning about cache memory. Understanding this concept is essential for computer architecture and performance optimization.
g.text: Cache Memory - Critical for CPU Performance
